intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution
causal_overclaim
unjustified_monotonicity
disallowed_extrapolations: Linear scaling of any hazard metric with SOC
Pressure amplification as primary secondary ignition effect

solver_view.stem: Four lithium-ion cell chemistries—NMC 1, NMC 2, LFP, and LTO—are subjected to identical heating abuse in a sealed canister. NMC cells exhibit onset temperatures of 140–145°C; LTO onset is 163°C; LFP onset is 200°C. Peak pressures: NMC 2 at ~102 bar, LFP at ~32 bar, LTO at ~29 bar. LFP vented gas contains the highest H2 fraction; NMC gas contains the highest CO fraction. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The LTO cell's lower onset temperature relative to LFP does not imply greater overall thermal hazard, because its peak pressure and peak temperature remain lower than LFP's.
B. The higher CO fraction in NMC vented gas compared to LFP is consistent with a greater contribution from cathode decomposition reactions in NMC cells.
C. Because NMC cells produce approximately three times the peak pressure of LFP cells, NMC cells present three times the explosion hazard in all confinement configurations.
D. The higher H2 fraction in LFP vented gas compared to NMC implies that LFP cells generate more total gas volume per cell than NMC cells under identical abuse conditions.
E. A lower thermal runaway onset temperature is a sufficient indicator of higher peak pressure during thermal runaway across these chemistries.
F. Gas composition differences between LFP and NMC chemistries suggest that different electrochemical decomposition pathways dominate during thermal runaway for each chemistry.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LTO onset (163°C) is lower than LFP (200°C) yet peak temperature (305°C) and peak pressure (28.8 bar) remain lower than LFP, so onset temperature does not predict overall hazard magnitude.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC produces highest CO, consistent with more extensive cathode decomposition releasing oxygen that reacts with carbon species; this is a minimal mechanistic inference supported by the chemistry difference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak pressure ratio applies under the stated confinement; generalizing explosion hazard to all confinement configurations is an unsupported scope expansion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher H2 fraction reflects composition, not total gas volume; total volume comparison across chemistries is not supported by the stated evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: LTO has lower onset than LFP but lower peak pressure; onset temperature alone is insufficient to predict peak pressure ranking.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Distinct gas species distributions between LFP and NMC are consistent with different dominant decomposition pathways, which is a minimal mechanistic inference from the composition data.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Decoupling of onset temperature from peak hazard metrics; gas composition as a decomposition pathway indicator; boundary of confinement-specific results
analysis.primary_boundary: Onset temperature is not a sufficient predictor of peak pressure or total hazard; composition fraction ≠ total volume
intended_error_modes: causal_overclaim
scope_expansion
measurement_scope_confusion
disallowed_extrapolations: Onset temperature ranking predicts peak pressure ranking
H2 fraction implies total gas volume ranking
Pressure ratio generalizes across confinement geometries

solver_view.stem: During recycling, four black mass samples (A, B, C, D) from lithium-ion batteries are evaluated for fire and explosion hazards. All samples exceed 400°C in a hot-air stream test. Only samples A and C produce self-sustaining smoldering, reaching above 640°C. Sample B initiates exothermic reaction at 170°C—lower than A and C—but smoldering does not propagate. Sample D produces 2.27 L H2/kg when contacted with 80°C water; sample A produces only 0.017 L H2/kg. None of the samples ignite in a dust explosion screening test. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. A lower exothermic onset temperature is not sufficient to ensure smoldering propagation; the total heat released must also exceed the threshold needed to sustain combustion.
B. Sample D poses a greater acute flammability hazard than sample A when contacted with hot water, due to substantially higher hydrogen generation per unit mass.
C. Because all four samples exceed 400°C in the hot-air stream test, all four samples present equivalent smoldering fire hazards under ambient storage conditions.
D. The failure of all samples to ignite in the dust explosion screening test implies that none of the samples will produce a flammable dust cloud under any industrial handling condition.
E. Samples A and C present a smoldering fire hazard that sample B does not, despite sample B having a lower exothermic onset temperature than A and C.

F. The hydrogen concentration from sample D reaching 3.54% by volume in the preliminary water contact test indicates that this sample could approach flammable concentration thresholds in a confined space with limited ventilation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Sample B has the lowest onset temperature yet fails to sustain smoldering, confirming onset temperature alone is insufficient; heat release adequacy determines propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Sample D produces ~134x more H2 per kg than sample A under identical water contact conditions, directly supporting greater acute flammability hazard.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Exceeding 400°C in a hot-air stream indicates spontaneous combustion tendency but not equivalent smoldering hazard; only A and C exhibit self-sustaining smoldering.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The screening test result applies to the specific test apparatus and energy conditions; generalizing to all industrial handling conditions is an unsupported scope expansion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly shows A and C sustain smoldering while B does not, despite B's lower onset temperature—a counterintuitive but directly supported conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: H2 concentration reaching 3.54% v/v is a measured result; in confined spaces with limited ventilation this could approach or reach flammable limits, a minimal engineering implication.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Decoupling of exothermic onset temperature from smoldering propagation; differential water-reactivity hazard; scope limits of screening test results
analysis.primary_boundary: Onset temperature does not predict smoldering propagation; screening test non-ignition does not generalize to all conditions
intended_error_modes: overgeneralization_from_screening_test
onset_temperature_as_hazard_proxy
scope_expansion
disallowed_extrapolations: All samples with >400°C equivalent smoldering hazard
Dust explosion screening result generalizes to all handling conditions

solver_view.prompt: An engineer is evaluating two scenarios involving NCM/graphite polymer lithium-ion cells under external heating abuse. In Scenario 1, a cell at 75% SOC vents without secondary ignition and produces a peak heat release rate of 4.38 kW and a maximum venting rate of 4.5 m/s. In Scenario 2, a cell at 100% SOC vents with secondary ignition, producing a peak heat release rate of 73.3 kW and a maximum venting rate of 9.4 m/s. Internal pressure at 75% SOC is 0.53 MPa and at 100% SOC is 1.39 MPa. The engineer proposes that reducing operating SOC from 100% to 75% is sufficient to manage all hazard dimensions proportionally. Evaluate this claim, identifying which hazard metrics change proportionally and which do not, and explain the engineering implication for hazard management strategy.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The claim that all hazard dimensions scale proportionally with SOC reduction from 100% to 75% is not admissible. Internal pressure decreases from 1.39 to 0.53 MPa (a ~62% reduction), and venting rate decreases from 9.4 to 4.5 m/s (~52% reduction)—these are meaningful but not proportional reductions. Most critically, peak heat release rate drops from 73.3 kW (with secondary ignition at 100% SOC) to 4.38 kW at 75% SOC—a reduction of over 93%. This disproportionate drop occurs because secondary ignition at 100% SOC involves gas-phase combustion that amplifies heat release far beyond what the electrochemical and thermal decomposition alone produce. The venting rate and pressure changes are moderate, but the heat release change is regime-crossing: secondary ignition represents a qualitatively different hazard mode, not merely a higher-SOC continuation of the same mechanism. The engineering implication is that SOC reduction to 75% may be highly effective at eliminating the catastrophic fire hazard associated with secondary ignition, but it does not proportionally reduce mechanical hazards such as internal pressure and venting momentum. A complete hazard management strategy must address both the gas combustion risk (which SOC reduction strongly mitigates) and the mechanical venting hazard (which persists at reduced but not eliminated levels at 75% SOC).
must_include: heat release rate reduction is disproportionately large due to secondary ignition being a qualitatively different mechanism
pressure and venting rate reductions are significant but not proportional to heat release reduction
secondary ignition at 100% SOC represents a regime change, not a continuous scaling
SOC reduction alone does not proportionally manage all hazard dimensions
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming that 75% SOC eliminates all thermal runaway risk
Claiming pressure scales linearly with SOC
Generalizing these results to other cell chemistries or formats without stated evidence

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Regime-crossing behavior of secondary ignition; disproportionate heat release amplification versus moderate pressure/venting changes; hazard management implications of SOC reduction
analysis.primary_boundary: Secondary ignition is a qualitative regime change, not a continuous SOC-proportional amplification; different hazard metrics respond differently to SOC reduction
intended_error_modes: Assuming all hazard metrics scale proportionally with SOC
Treating secondary ignition as a continuous amplification rather than a regime transition
Concluding SOC reduction to 75% eliminates thermal hazard rather than eliminating the secondary ignition regime

solver_view.stem: An NCM622 battery module operates in a closed enclosure. An engineer observes that reducing SOC from 100% to 50% simultaneously increases average propagation time between cells and shifts gas composition toward higher CO2 fraction with sharply reduced CO and C2H4. The engineer must assess which conclusions about the hazard profile at 50% SOC are defensible. Which of the following statements are supported by this evidence?
options: A. The increase in propagation time at 50% SOC indicates the thermal energy available to trigger adjacent cells is reduced, but this does not mean the flammability hazard of the vented gas is proportionally reduced.
B. The shift toward higher CO2 and lower CO at 50% SOC implies that the combustion reactions within the cell are more complete, making the vented gas mixture less flammable than at 100% SOC.
C. Because total gas moles decrease substantially from 100% to 50% SOC, the explosive limit range of the mixed gas at 50% SOC remains approximately 5 times that of methane.

D. The reduction in C2H4 content with decreasing SOC is consistent with reduced availability of electrolyte decomposition pathways, but the CO2 increase alone cannot confirm complete suppression of ignition risk.
E. At 50% SOC in a closed space, propagation between cells is slower but still occurs, so the module cannot be considered safe from thermal runaway propagation at this charge level.
F. The observed gas composition shift at lower SOC is sufficient to conclude that the vented gas mixture at 50% SOC falls entirely outside the flammable range.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Propagation time increase reflects reduced thermal driving force; gas flammability depends on composition, not propagation rate alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher CO2 fraction does not indicate more complete combustion inside the cell; it reflects changed reaction pathways. Flammability conclusion is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The ~5x explosive limit range relative to CH4 is stated only for SOC above 50%; applying it to 50% SOC violates the stated threshold condition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C2H4 reduction is consistent with reduced decomposition pathways; CO2 increase alone is insufficient to confirm full ignition suppression without flammability data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states no propagation below 25% SOC; at 50% SOC propagation still occurs, only more slowly. The module is not safe from propagation at 50%.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas composition shift is described but no evidence places the 50% SOC mixture entirely outside the flammable range; this is an unsupported extrapolation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of hazard conclusions at intermediate SOC given composition and propagation evidence
analysis.primary_boundary: The ~5x explosive limit range applies only above 50% SOC; propagation suppression threshold is below 25% SOC, not at 50%
intended_error_modes: condition violation on explosive limit claim
causal overclaim linking CO2 increase to flammability suppression
scope expansion declaring gas non-flammable
disallowed_extrapolations: Applying the 5x explosive limit range to 50% SOC
Concluding 50% SOC is safe from propagation
Inferring complete flammability elimination from composition shift

solver_view.stem: A battery storage facility engineer is evaluating three cell chemistries—LFP, NTM (sodium-ion), and NCM—for a thermally sensitive installation. Available data: NTM has the lowest activation energy among the three; NTM and LFP have nearly identical TNT-equivalent energy release during thermal runaway; NCM releases approximately 2.2 times more TNT-equivalent energy than NTM; NTM's self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT) increases most steeply with improved surface cooling among the three types. Which conclusions are defensible for this installation?
options: A. NTM cells are more thermally reactive than LFP and NCM cells under identical storage conditions, because their lower activation energy means exothermic reactions initiate at lower temperatures.

B. Improving forced convective cooling at the cell surface yields a greater SADT benefit for NTM than for LFP or NCM, making enhanced cooling a more effective mitigation strategy for NTM in this context.
C. Because NTM and LFP release nearly equal TNT-equivalent energy, they pose equivalent blast hazard in all failure scenarios, and NCM poses approximately 2.2 times greater blast hazard than either.

D. An NCM installation requires more stringent blast-consequence planning than an equivalent NTM or LFP installation, but NTM's lower activation energy means its ignition prevention requirements are at least as stringent as NCM's.

E. The NTM battery's intermediate maximum temperature during thermal runaway compared to LFP and NCM implies it will always propagate thermal runaway to adjacent cells at a rate between those of LFP and NCM.
F. Under natural convection storage, NTM cells have a lower spontaneous ignition threshold temperature than LFP cells, consistent with their lower activation energy.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower activation energy directly implies exothermic reactions initiate at lower temperatures, confirming higher thermal reactivity for NTM over both LFP and NCM.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NTM SADT increases most steeply with U among the three types, so improved surface cooling provides the greatest SADT benefit for NTM.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TNT-equivalent energy equality applies to the specific tested cells; claiming equivalence across all failure scenarios is an unsupported universal generalization.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NCM's higher TNT energy requires more blast planning; NTM's lower activation energy means thermal ignition prevention is at least as critical as for NCM despite lower blast energy.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Intermediate maximum temperature does not imply intermediate propagation rate; propagation depends on multiple factors not reducible to peak temperature alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 169.6°C spontaneous ignition threshold is given for NTM; no equivalent LFP threshold is provided in evidence, making the comparison unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-chemistry safety trade-off: thermal reactivity vs. energy release vs. cooling responsiveness
analysis.primary_boundary: Lower activation energy implies higher thermal reactivity but does not determine blast consequence; TNT equivalence is cell-specific, not universal
intended_error_modes: overgeneralizing TNT equivalence to all scenarios
reducing propagation rate to peak temperature alone
inferring LFP ignition threshold from NTM data
disallowed_extrapolations: Universal blast equivalence from single-cell TNT data
Propagation rate ordering from temperature ordering
LFP spontaneous ignition temperature from NTM evidence

solver_view.stem: Two LFP cell formats—a larger-capacity cell (LFP-L) and a smaller-capacity cell (LFP-SS)—are tested under thermal runaway conditions. At 100% SOC, LFP-SS produces more gas per unit capacity than LFP-L across all test environments, yet LFP-L reaches a higher maximum temperature. At 110% SOC, the upper explosive limit (UEL) of LFP-SS gas becomes lower than that of LFP-L, attributed to further decomposition of higher hydrocarbons into low-carbon species. An engineer must select which statements about these two formats are admissible.
options: A. At 100% SOC, LFP-SS presents a wider flammable concentration range than LFP-L because both its LEL and UEL are higher, which directly implies greater ignition probability in a vented enclosure.

B. The higher maximum temperature of LFP-L at both 100% and 110% SOC, combined with lower gas production per unit capacity, suggests that LFP-L stores more energy in forms that manifest as heat rather than gas-phase products.

C. At 110% SOC, LFP-L gas has a higher UEL than LFP-SS, which is counterintuitive given LFP-SS produces more gas per capacity, and is explained by the composition shift toward low-carbon hydrocarbons in LFP-SS at elevated SOC.
D. Because LFP-SS produces more gas per capacity than LFP-L under all tested conditions, LFP-SS will always generate a larger absolute gas volume in any real installation regardless of cell capacity.

E. The presence of oxygen in an air environment increases peak pressure change for LFP-SS compared to nitrogen, indicating that enclosure atmosphere is a relevant variable in pressure hazard assessment for this cell format.
F. The reversal of UEL ordering between 100% and 110% SOC demonstrates that flammability limit comparisons between cell formats are SOC-dependent and cannot be generalized from a single charge level.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher LEL and UEL for LFP-SS does not directly imply greater ignition probability; a wider flammable range requires LEL lower and UEL higher, not both shifted upward.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP-L has higher peak temperature and lower gas per capacity; this pattern is consistent with energy partitioning favoring thermal over gas-phase release.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 110% SOC, LFP-SS UEL drops below LFP-L due to CxHy decomposition into low-carbon species in LFP-SS; this counterintuitive reversal is mechanistically explained.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas production per capacity is higher for LFP-SS, but absolute volume depends on total cell capacity; larger LFP-L cells could produce more total gas despite lower per-capacity rate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Air environment yields higher pressure change than N2 for LFP-SS at 100% SOC, confirming enclosure atmosphere affects pressure hazard.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: UEL ordering reverses between 100% and 110% SOC, directly demonstrating that flammability comparisons between formats are SOC-dependent.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: SOC-dependent flammability limit reversal and format-dependent energy partitioning between heat and gas
analysis.primary_boundary: Higher LEL and UEL together do not imply wider flammable range; per-capacity gas rate cannot be extrapolated to absolute volume without knowing cell capacity
intended_error_modes: misinterpreting shifted LEL/UEL as wider flammable range
extrapolating per-capacity rate to absolute volume across cell sizes
disallowed_extrapolations: Absolute gas volume from per-capacity rate without capacity information
Universal flammability ordering from single SOC data point

solver_view.prompt: A safety engineer is comparing NTM (sodium-ion) and NCM lithium-ion cells for use in a confined energy storage room. The NTM cell has a lower activation energy than NCM, a lower TNT-equivalent energy release, but its self-accelerating decomposition temperature (SADT) increases more steeply with surface heat transfer coefficient than NCM. The engineer proposes that NTM is the safer choice in all respects because it releases less blast energy. Identify the specific flaw in this reasoning and explain what additional safety dimension the engineer has failed to account for, referencing the relevant physical mechanisms.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's reasoning is flawed because it conflates blast energy release with overall thermal safety. While NTM does release less TNT-equivalent energy than NCM, its lower activation energy means exothermic decomposition reactions are initiated at lower temperatures, making NTM more thermally reactive and more susceptible to spontaneous ignition at lower storage temperatures. This means ignition prevention requirements for NTM are at least as stringent as for NCM despite the lower blast consequence. The engineer has failed to account for the initiation hazard dimension: a system can pose lower consequence per event but higher likelihood of event initiation. Additionally, the steep SADT dependence on surface cooling for NTM means that inadequate cooling has a more severe effect on NTM safety margins than on NCM, introducing a cooling-system reliability requirement that the engineer's blast-energy comparison does not capture.
must_include: lower activation energy implies thermal reactions initiate at lower temperatures for NTM
lower blast energy does not mean lower ignition or initiation risk
NTM ignition prevention requirements are at least as stringent as NCM
SADT of NTM is more sensitive to surface cooling conditions than NCM
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming NTM is more dangerous than NCM in all respects
Assigning a specific spontaneous ignition temperature to NCM from NTM evidence
Concluding NTM will always ignite before NCM under identical conditions without specifying temperature regime

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing initiation hazard (activation energy, SADT) from consequence hazard (TNT-equivalent energy) in multi-chemistry safety comparison
analysis.primary_boundary: Lower blast energy does not imply lower overall hazard when activation energy and SADT sensitivity create a higher initiation risk
intended_error_modes: single-metric safety ranking using only blast energy
ignoring activation energy as an initiation hazard indicator
ignoring cooling-system sensitivity as a safety dimension

solver_view.stem: A 280 Ah LiFePO4 prismatic cell undergoes thermal runaway triggered by external heating. Engineers measure maximum cell temperature, total gas volume, and vent gas composition at 25%, 50%, and 75% SOC. They observe that the flammable range of the vent gas mixture is widest at 50% SOC, not 75% SOC, despite 75% SOC producing far greater gas volumes and higher temperatures. Which of the following conclusions are supported by this observation?
options: A. The flammability hazard posed by vent gases does not scale monotonically with SOC because gas composition shifts—specifically the marked decrease in CO2 fraction and increase in H2 fraction at higher SOC—alter the flammability window in a non-additive way.
B. Since 75% SOC produces the highest gas temperature and volume, it necessarily presents the greatest deflagration risk, and the wider flammable range at 50% SOC is an artifact of measurement error.

C. The reduction in CO2 fraction at 75% SOC relative to 50% SOC reduces the fire-retardant contribution within the gas mixture, which narrows the upper flammability boundary even as total combustible gas volume increases.

D. Because the lower flammability limit at 75% SOC is lower than at 50% SOC, ignition of the 75% SOC vent gas requires a higher ambient gas concentration to initiate, making it safer in confined spaces.

E. A single hazard metric combining gas volume and flammable range would rank 50% SOC as posing greater deflagration risk than 75% SOC, even though 75% SOC generates more than seven times the gas volume of 25% SOC.
F. The non-monotonic flammability behavior implies that thermal runaway severity, measured by peak temperature, is a reliable proxy for deflagration risk across all SOC levels.

G. At 75% SOC, the upper flammability limit contracts substantially compared to 50% SOC, meaning that even though ignition is easier, the concentration window over which violent deflagration can occur is significantly narrower.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: FHI order 50%>75%>25% and CO2/H2 composition shifts confirm non-monotonic flammability with SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Directly contradicts evidence that FHI and flammable range do not peak at 75% SOC; measurement artifact claim is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CO2 fraction drops from 40% at 50% SOC to 12% at 75% SOC, reducing fire-retardant effect and narrowing UFL window.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lower LFL at 75% SOC (~5%) means ignition requires less gas concentration, not more—the reasoning is inverted.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Combining volume and flammable range into a single ranking is not supported; evidence addresses FHI and RF separately without authorizing composite ranking.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly shows peak temperature is not a reliable proxy for deflagration risk; FHI is non-monotonic with SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct

solver_view.stem: LiFePO4 battery thermal runaway gases are released into a confined space that also contains leaking methane. At 75% SOC, the TR gas H2 fraction is approximately 26% and CO2 fraction is approximately 12%. An engineer evaluates explosion risk as methane concentration in the space rises from 2% to 12%. Explosion overpressure peaks between 4–6% methane, while free radical concentration peaks at 8–9% methane. Which statements are admissible given these conditions?
options: A. The concentration at which explosion overpressure peaks is lower than the concentration at which free radical activity peaks, indicating that macroscopic pressure buildup and chain-branching radical generation are temporally decoupled.
B. At methane concentrations above 8%, the explosion overpressure continues to rise because higher radical concentrations drive more complete combustion.

C. The decrease in CO2 fraction at 75% SOC relative to lower SOC conditions reduces the fire-retardant buffering capacity of the TR gas, making the mixture more susceptible to ignition at lower methane additions.
D. Post-explosion exhaust at methane concentrations exceeding 8% will contain elevated CO relative to CO2, because oxygen deficiency shifts combustion toward incomplete oxidation pathways.
E. Because peak overpressure occurs at 4–6% methane, the hazard is fully controlled by ensuring methane concentration stays below 4%, with no residual explosion risk from the TR gas alone.
F. The fire-retardant effect of CO2 in the TR gas is sufficient at 75% SOC to prevent ignition of the mixture regardless of methane concentration.

G. The temporal separation between peak radical concentration and peak overpressure implies that using radical concentration as the sole predictor of explosion severity would overestimate risk at methane concentrations above the overpressure peak.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states peak overpressure at 4–6% methane while radical peak is at 8–9%, confirming temporal decoupling.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overpressure peaks at 4–6% and declines above that; higher radical concentration above 8% does not sustain rising overpressure—oxygen deficiency limits combustion.
option_judgments.error_type: causal overclaim / ignoring oxygen limitation regime

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CO2 drops from ~40% at 50% SOC to ~12% at 75% SOC; reduced CO2 lowers fire-retardant buffering, increasing susceptibility to ignition with methane addition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that above 8% methane, oxygen deficiency causes incomplete combustion with sharp CO increase and CO2 reduction in exhaust.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: TR gas itself has an LEL of ~5–11%; explosion risk exists from TR gas alone even without methane, so staying below 4% methane does not eliminate hazard.
option_judgments.error_type: protective mechanism overclaim / ignoring TR gas baseline flammability

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: CO2 at 75% SOC is only ~12%; evidence shows UEL expands significantly at high SOC, meaning ignition is not prevented by residual CO2.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Temporal decoupling means radical peak at 8–9% does not correspond to overpressure peak at 4–6%; using radicals alone overestimates severity above the overpressure peak.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Decoupling between free radical concentration and explosion overpressure; regime change above oxygen-deficient threshold
analysis.primary_boundary: Peak overpressure occurs at lower methane concentration than peak radical activity; above 8% methane, oxygen deficiency dominates and shifts hazard character
intended_error_modes: Assuming radical concentration directly predicts overpressure
Claiming methane control below 4% eliminates all explosion risk
Assuming CO2 fraction at 75% SOC is sufficient to prevent ignition
disallowed_extrapolations: Monotonic relationship between methane concentration and explosion severity
Radical concentration as sole explosion severity predictor
CO2 fraction at 75% SOC providing complete fire suppression

solver_view.stem: Two 314 Ah LiFePO4 batteries undergo thermal runaway—one via overheating, one via overcharging. Both produce gas mixtures dominated by H2, CO2, and CO. The overheating gas reaches maximum explosion overpressure at a concentration of 15.86%, substantially below the calculated stoichiometric concentration of 28.19%. Engineers propose that this gap is diagnostic of a specific suppression mechanism within the gas mixture itself. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The deviation of the actual Pmax concentration from stoichiometric is consistent with CO and CO2 within the TR gas interfering with chain-branching reactions, preventing complete combustion even at the prescribed test conditions.

B. Because overcharging generates approximately 26% more total gas volume than overheating, the overcharging gas mixture will always produce higher maximum explosion overpressure than the overheating gas mixture.

C. The suppressive effect of CO2 and CO on chain reaction propagation implies that increasing H2 fraction—as occurs at higher SOC—would reduce the gap between actual and stoichiometric Pmax concentrations.

D. Post-explosion residual gas after a lean-oxygen event contains elevated CO relative to pre-explosion levels, which itself constitutes a secondary flammable hazard since CO has a non-negligible flammability range.

E. The Pmax concentration gap between actual (15.86%) and stoichiometric (28.19%) proves that the TR gas cannot explode at any concentration above 15.86%, since the suppressive components dominate at higher concentrations.

F. The consumption order of combustible gases during explosion—where hydrocarbons are consumed before H2, and H2 before CO—is consistent with differences in the reactivity of hydrogen atoms across these species.

G. Since both overheating and overcharging TR gases contain CO2 and CO, both mixtures will exhibit Pmax concentration values below their respective stoichiometric concentrations, though the magnitude of the gap may differ.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes the stoichiometric gap to suppressive effects of CO2 and CO inhibiting chain reaction mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Greater gas volume does not determine Pmax; composition and chain reaction dynamics govern overpressure, not total volume alone.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher SOC increases H2 fraction and decreases CO2 fraction; reduced CO2 suppression would shrink the gap between actual and stoichiometric Pmax concentration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows CO increases 280.95% post-explosion in lean-oxygen state; CO is a flammable gas, constituting a secondary explosion hazard.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Pmax occurring at 15.86% does not mean explosion is impossible above that concentration; it means the chain reaction is suppressed at stoichiometric conditions, not that higher concentrations are inert.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Gas consumption order C2H6>C2H4>CH4>H2>CO negatively correlates with Mulliken charge of hydrogen atoms, consistent with reactivity differences.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Both TR gas types contain CO2 and CO; the suppression mechanism applies to both, though proportions differ, so both Pmax concentrations should fall below stoichiometric.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism by which gas composition shifts the actual Pmax concentration below stoichiometric; secondary hazard from post-explosion CO enrichment
analysis.primary_boundary: Pmax concentration below stoichiometric indicates suppression, not inertness above that concentration
intended_error_modes: Equating greater gas volume with higher Pmax
Interpreting Pmax concentration as an upper ignition boundary
Ignoring post-explosion CO as a secondary flammable hazard
disallowed_extrapolations: Gas volume as determinant of explosion overpressure
Pmax concentration as upper safe limit for explosion risk
Suppression mechanism preventing all combustion above Pmax concentration

solver_view.prompt: A battery safety engineer is evaluating vented gases from a 280 Ah LiFePO4 cell at 75% SOC following thermal runaway. The engineer notes that the lower flammability limit of the vent gas is approximately 5%—lower than at 50% SOC—and concludes that 75% SOC presents the greatest overall flammability hazard. A colleague argues this conclusion is incomplete. Explain, using the relationship between gas composition, flammability limits, and flammable range, why the colleague's objection is technically valid. Identify the specific compositional mechanism responsible and state which SOC level presents the greatest deflagration risk according to the available evidence.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The engineer's conclusion is incomplete because a lower LFL indicates easier ignition but does not capture the full flammable range. At 75% SOC, the upper flammability limit contracts sharply to approximately 4.7–6.6%, compared to 55–65% at 50% SOC. This means the concentration window over which sustained deflagration can occur is approximately 17.6% at 75% SOC versus approximately 48.5% at 50% SOC. The compositional mechanism is the marked decrease in CO2 fraction (from ~40% at 50% SOC to ~12% at 75% SOC) and the corresponding increase in H2 fraction. CO2 acts as a fire-retardant component; its reduction at high SOC alters the combustion boundary structure, narrowing the UFL substantially even as H2 content rises. The Flammability Hazard Index and flammable range both indicate that 50% SOC presents the greatest deflagration risk, not 75% SOC, despite 75% SOC producing far greater gas volumes and higher temperatures. Thermal severity and deflagration risk are therefore not equivalent metrics.
must_include: Lower LFL at 75% SOC indicates easier ignition but does not represent overall flammability hazard
Upper flammability limit at 75% SOC contracts to approximately 4.7–6.6%, far below 50% SOC value of 55–65%
Flammable range at 75% SOC (~17.6%) is smaller than at 50% SOC (~48.5%)
CO2 fraction decreases from ~40% at 50% SOC to ~12% at 75% SOC, reducing fire-retardant buffering
50% SOC presents the greatest deflagration risk based on flammable range and FHI
Thermal severity (peak temperature, gas volume) is not a reliable proxy for deflagration risk
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming 75% SOC is safe because its flammable range is narrow
Attributing UFL contraction to H2 increase alone without referencing CO2 decrease
Generalizing the non-monotonic SOC-flammability relationship to all battery chemistries
Using gas volume as a determinant of deflagration risk

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinction between LFL-based ignition ease and flammable range as a measure of deflagration risk; compositional mechanism for non-monotonic SOC-flammability relationship
analysis.primary_boundary: LFL alone is insufficient to characterize overall flammability hazard; UFL contraction at 75% SOC narrows the deflagration window despite lower LFL
intended_error_modes: Equating lower LFL with greater overall flammability hazard
Assuming flammability risk increases monotonically with SOC
Ignoring UFL contraction as a counterbalancing factor
Attributing hazard ranking solely to gas volume or peak temperature
